items.title: Why thyrotoxicosis is not synonymous with hyperthyroidism
items.content:
Thyrotoxicosis denotes the clinical and biochemical state of excess circulating thyroid hormone from any cause. Hyperthyroidism specifically refers to hormone overproduction by an overactive thyroid gland.

This distinction drives workup: destructive thyroiditis, factitious ingestion, and iodine-induced thyrotoxicosis can present with suppressed TSH and elevated free T4/T3 yet show low radioiodine uptake — they are not hyperthyroidism and should not receive antithyroid drugs or radioiodine as first-line therapy.
keyPoints: High uptake → true hyperthyroidism (Graves', toxic adenoma, toxic MNG).
Low uptake → thyroiditis, factitia, iodine excess.
items.reference: THYROTOXICOSIS: "Thyrotoxicosis is defined as the state of thyroid hormone excess and is not synonymous with hyperthyroidism, which is the result of excessive thyroid function."

items.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with palpitations, weight loss, and a diffuse goiter. TSH is suppressed, free T4 is elevated, and TRAb is positive. Radionuclide uptake is diffusely increased. Which finding best supports Graves' disease over subacute thyroiditis?
items.explanation: Graves' disease causes diffuse high uptake due to TSH-receptor stimulation. Thyroiditis typically shows low uptake with inflammatory markers and often a tender gland.
items.reference: Differential Diagnosis: "a radionuclide scan and uptake will distinguish the diffuse, high uptake of Graves' disease from destructive thyroiditis."
